"Shaykh Ṣāliḥ al-Fawzān speaks about those who allow the disbelievers into the lands of muslims when assisting them against the muslims and how this is disbelief even if he hates their religion.

Interestingly, he then speaks about those who bring them in and enabled them to build churches and the likes, and this is what he says :

_

Shaykh Sulaymān ibn ‘Abd Allāh ibn Muḥammad ibn ‘Abd al Wahhāb said: “[That if someone openly displays their] agreement with their religion [of the Mushrikin]”

Shaykh Ṣāliḥ al-Fawzān said: “Meaning: He enabled them [the mushrikin] to establish churches in the lands of the Muslims, and granted them freedom, and said to the Muslims: “Do not prevent them from anything; whatever they request from you, comply with it.” This is mudāhanah, because our religion forbids this.”

…

Shaykh Sulaymān ibn ‘Abd Allāh ibn Muḥammad ibn ‘Abd al-Wahhāb said: “[And if he does this then] indeed he is a disbeliever like them.”

Shaykh Ṣāliḥ al-Fawzān commentates: “Because he enabled the [religion of the] disbelievers and was pleased with their religion, and he forewent what the religion of Islām obliged upon him — namely disassociating the religion of the disbelievers and disavowing it. He did not disavow it; rather, he permitted it, justified it, and granted them freedom [to establish their religion] in the lands of the Muslims.”

Shaykh Sulaymān ibn ‘Abd Allāh ibn Muḥammad ibn ‘Abd al-Wahhāb said: “[And if he does this then indeed he is a disbeliever like them] even if he detests their religion and hates them.”

Shaykh Ṣāliḥ al-Fawzān commentates: “Meaning: if he loves their religion, then this makes him a disbeliever (kāfir), an apostate, if he establish them. And if he establishes them while he hates their religion, then this act of his constitutes apostasy from Islām, because he has assisted them against the Muslims.”

شرح رسالة الدلائل في حكم موالاة أهل الإشراك (31,32)"

"As Muslims, we must stand firm upon our faith and reject those who falsely claim to represent us. Iran, often portrayed as a defender of Muslim causes, cannot and must not be supported, because:

1️⃣ Their belief system has no connection to Islam. Their ʿaqīdah strays far from Tawḥīd and slips into shirk, by elevating their Imams to near-divine status and seeking intercession through them in ways that clearly contradict the oneness of Allah. Their deep rooted hatred towards the Prophet Muḥammad’s (peace be upon him) noble companions and honorable wives, especially ʿĀ’ishah (may Allah be Pleased with her), is a major red line that no sincere Muslim can ignore.

2️⃣ Iran has the blood of countless Muslims on its hands. In fact, its crimes in places like Syria and Yemen have caused more death and destruction than even Israel.

🛑 Our stance is not to support either side. Rather, their internal conflict weakens their grip on the region while keeping them preoccupied with themselves. This may give our brothers and sisters in Gaza and elsewhere some breathing space and relief.

Some ask: “Why not support Iran, when the so-called Sunni Arab countries are doing nothing for Gaza?”
The truth is, even these regimes have nothing to do with Islam. They are secular puppets of America and Israel. The ordinary people (awām) in these nations are either powerless, or live in fear, trapped behind blocked nationalistic borders. Their rulers serve foreign interests. Not the Ummah.

Had these countries been ruled by sincere Muslims, united in Tawḥīd and loyalty to Allah, the Zionist entity Israel would have collapsed long ago. Instead, they remain idle while Gaza bleeds proving their betrayal beyond any doubt.

As Muslims, we must reject both Iran’s deviant sectarian agenda and the treachery of pro-Western regimes."

""Wahhabism is the tool of extremism and modern day terrorism. 

It began as theology fused with power.

Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab was an 18th-century cleric from Najd who preached uncompromising monotheism and condemned what he saw as religious deviation. But his defining weapon was not spirituality. It was takfir: declaring other Muslims outside the fold.

And even inside his own home, there was alarm.

His father, Abd al-Wahhab ibn Sulayman, reportedly warned people not to follow his son’s extremism. His own brother, Sulayman ibn Abd al-Wahhab, wrote an entire refutation:

📖 الصواعق الإلهية في الرد على الوهابية
Al-Sawa‘iq al-Ilahiyya fi al-Radd ‘ala al-Wahhabiyya
(“Divine Thunderbolts in Refutation of the Wahhabis”)

In it, he warned that reckless takfir leads to bloodshed. He compared the movement to early Kharijite extremism — religion weaponized against Muslims. And, that is exactly what happened in the following years.

Then came 1744.

The pact between Ibn Abd al-Wahhab and Muhammad bin Saud in Diriyah.

The formula was simple and ruthless:

Al-Sheikh holds religion.
Al-Saud holds power.

Through that alliance, theology became state machinery.

Expansion was framed as purification. Campaigns like the attack on Karbala in 1802 are documented in multiple chronicles. Sectarian violence was justified as religious correction. Unity meant obedience. Dissent meant deviance.

That fusion : Religion as political legitimacy became the backbone of the Saudi state model.

Fast forward to the 20th century.

Oil wealth globalized the doctrine. Religious institutions expanded. Clerical authority was centralized. Universities such as Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University became exporters of a rigid interpretation of Salafism.

Then came Afghanistan.

During the Soviet war, Saudi religious rhetoric aligned with U.S. anti-communist strategy. “Arab Afghans” were mobilized under religious banners. The ideological infrastructure created centuries earlier proved adaptable to modern geopolitics.

From that environment emerged figures like Osama bin Laden, founder of Al-Qaeda. Later movements from Al-Qaeda to ISIS drew heavily from Takfiri Wahhabi logic: excommunication as a political weapon to justify jihad and murder. 

This does not mean Ibn Abd al-Wahhab sat in the 18th century planning 9/11.

It means that when you normalize declaring Muslims apostates, and when state power protects that logic, you build an intellectual ecosystem extremists can later radicalize further.

The 9/11 hijackers, European terror cells, sectarian militias in Iraq and Syria — they justified violence using theological frameworks built on takfir and militant “purification.”

That ideological pipeline matters.

Throughout modern Middle Eastern politics, religion was repeatedly used as leverage:

Against Arab nationalism.
Against Jamal Abdulnasser.
During Cold War alignments.
During regional rivalries.

Each time, theology served strategy.

And now?

When Saudi enters political conflict whether in Yemen, Qatar, or against the UAE — the instinctive tool is familiar: religious framing. Accuse. Label. Delegitimize.

Same method. New arena.

History shows the pattern:

When religion becomes state armor, power hides behind piety.
When dissent becomes heresy, control becomes sacred.
And when takfir becomes political currency, violence follows.

The earliest warning came from inside the family itself. But were silenced so Bin Saud and Alsheikh can carry out their gruesome acts of genocide against the Shia people in Alhassa and then in 1802, Saudi-Wahhabi forces attacked Karbala and killed under the justification of Takfir."
